rulings.Case Title: TONY FU VS. DEMAS YAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Cross-Complainant Kaman Lius Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Order Dismissing Cross-Complaint On 2023-03-27
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for May 17, 2023 line 2.\ DEFENDANT KAMAN LIU Cross-Complainant Kaman Lius Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Order Dismissing Cross-Complaint On 2023-03-27 is DENIED. However, on its own motion, the Court vacates its March 27, 2023 ruling dismissing the Liu's Cross-Complaint, and reinstates the cross-complaint as to Tony Fu, Crystal Lei, and Bryant Fu. In their Motion to Dismiss the Cross-Complaint, Crystal Lei and Bryant Fu, claimed that the Bankruptcy Court had issued a Discharge Order on March 29, 2019 prohibiting the continued prosecution of Liu's Cross-Complaint against Tony Fu. It has come to the Court's attention that a subsequent Order, dated September 25, 2020, states the exact opposite: that Liu's cross-claims, as potential set-offs, do not violate the discharge injunction. (Final Order Re: Discharged Debtor's Motion for Sanctions, Case No. 17-41205CN, pp. 5-7.) With respect to the Bankruptcy Stay filed April 26, 2017, it applied as to all cross-defendants not just the debtor, in this case, Tony Fu. The automatic stay applies to a creditor's fraudulent transfer action against a transferee from the debtor to the extent the action seeks satisfaction of a claim from the debtor. (In re Koeberer (9th Cir. BAP 2021) 632 BR 680, 687-688.) That is exactly what Liu's cross-complaint is all about-fraudulent transfers from Tony Fu to his wife Crystal Lei and his son Bryant Fu. (Cross-complaint, 11-13.) Based on the March 29, 2019 Discharge Order (which the Court never received a copy of), the 5-year deadline expires on September 14, 2024. The Court used the March 29, 2019 date, it being the most conservative approach to ascertain the 5-year deadline. Nonetheless, the parties are ordered to obtain and file the relevant document(s) this Court needs to determine the 5-year statutory deadline within 30 days of today's hearing. =(501/CFH) Parties may appear in-person, telephonically or via Zoom (Video - Webinar ID: 160 560 5023; Password: 172849; or Phone Dial in: (551) 285-1373; Webinar ID: 160 560 5023; Password: 172849). Parties who intend to appear at the hearing must give notice to opposing parties and the court promptly, but no later than 4:00 p.m. the court day before the hearing unless the tentative ruling has specified that a hearing is required. Notice of contesting a tentative ruling shall be provided by sending an email to the court to Department501ContestTR@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO VS. RICHARD KIWATA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition For Confirmation Of Referees Report Re Apportionment Of Fees And Expenses And Distribution Of Proceeds Of The Sale Of Real Property Discharge Of Referee
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for May 17, 2023 line 3. OTHER GERARD KEENA II Petition For Confirmation Of Referees Report Re Apportionment Of Fees And Expenses And Distribution Of Proceeds Of The Sale Of Real Property Discharge Of Referee is continued to June 2, 2023. Referee Keena to provide the Court with a supplemental brief, accompanied by appropriate declarations. The supplemental brief is required to explain the math behind the proposed distributions to Harold and Richard Kiwata. (1) What is meant by "Total Funds?" (Petition, 7:9-10.) (2) Why are the apportionment costs and fees of Richard and Howard subtracted by one another? (Petition, 7:5-10.) (3) If $1,554,060.18 remains in the total funds, and each party has a 50% interest, why is Richard receiving $328,954.07, despite being apportioned over $1 million of the costs and fees? (4) Why is Howard apportioned $1,225.106.11? (5) Please explain the significance, or lack thereof, of the September 14, 2021, order approving receiver's final report and accounting, in which the Court instructed Receiver to pay Plaintiff $225,000 under the settlement agreement, and its inclusion in the Referee's apportionment and distribution. (6) Please explain the significance of the May 11, 2023 Judgment for $1,010,351.62, and how it affects this distribution (this appears to be the law of the case).=(501/CFH) Parties may appear in-person, telephonically or via Zoom (Video - Webinar ID: 160 560 5023; Password: 172849; or Phone Dial in: (551) 285-1373; Webinar ID: 160 560 5023; Password: 172849). rulings.Case Title: MARK BECHELLI ET AL VS. JOHN C. KILROY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT JOHN KILROY, KILROY REALTY CORPORATION, KR FLOWER MART, LLC, KR CFM, INC., KILROY REALTY, L.P. DEFENDANTS' NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO DISQUALIFY COUNSEL
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for May 18, 2023 line 4. DEFENDANT JOHN KILROY, KILROY REALTY CORPORATION, KR FLOWER MART, LLC, KR CFM, INC., KILROY REALTY, L.P. DEFENDANTS' NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO DISQUALIFY COUNSEL Moreover, when the Court considers the financial burden and hardship of replacing disqualified counsel, there is absolutely no proportionality when balanced against the Court's findings that the privileged information was not used in a way that has or that can affect the litigation. (See, SpeeDee Oil, supra, 20 Cal.4th at 1144-45.) Plaintiff's counsel took this case on at a greatly reduced hourly rate, hoping to recover its fees if successful. But this case does not merely involve Plaintiff's claims, but also Defendant's crossclaims against Plaintiff that assert damages of upwards of $500,000. Plaintiff asserts that they have exhausted a home equity line to keep up with the financing of this litigation. To alleviate some pressure, Plaintiff's counsel agreed that for the first five months of 2023, his firm would only collect a flat stipend per month, covering about 15-20% of fees, if billed at customary rates. In addition to the financial burden, disqualification would result not only in Plaintiff being deprived of counsel of their choice, but also of any competent counsel. Defendant filed their motion approximately one month before trial. Plaintiff's counsel has estimated that it would take replacement counsel at least 500 hours to learn the documents in Evidence. The case involves understanding a multi-billion dollar real estate development; the interactions, including political, with the City officials; intense Flower Mart tenant negotiations over six years; design issues; COVID policies; merger documents; and massive and complex sublease documents. Knowledge of 40,0000 pages of properly produced documents have already been digested by the attorneys in this case. It would take another 500 hours - modestly estimated - for a new firm to prepare for trial and try the case. Counsel estimates costs would involve a minimum of $100,000, and attorneys fees could range anywhere from $800,000 for a market-driven firm, if Plaintiff could find one willing to take this on; a larger firm would charge close to $1.2 million. In essence, granting this motion for disqualification would amount to terminating sanctions and Plaintiff's would thereby lose what they have invested in this case plus their expected verdict or settlement value. In this case, Plaintiff's right to representation should not be nullified merely because Defendant's inadvertence "put an adversary's confidences in an attorney's mailbox." (State Fund, supra, 70 Cal.App.4th at 657.)=(501/CFH) Part 2 of 2 Parties may appear in-person, telephonically or via Zoom (Video - Webinar ID: 160 560 5023; Password: 172849; or Phone Dial in: (551) 285-1373; Webinar ID: 160 560 5023; Password: 172849). Parties who intend to appear at the hearing must give notice to opposing parties and the court promptly, but no later than 4:00 p.m. the court day before the hearing unless the tentative ruling has specified that a hearing is required. Notice of contesting a tentative ruling shall be provided by sending an email to the court to Department501ContestTR@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. A party may not argue at the hearing if the opposing party is not so notified, and the opposing party does not appear.

rulings.Case Title: MARK BECHELLI ET AL VS. JOHN C. KILROY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for May 17, 2023 line 4. DEFENDANT JOHN KILROY, KILROY REALTY CORPORATION, KR FLOWER MART, LLC, KR CFM, INC., KILROY REALTY, L.P. Defendants' Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Counsel is DENIED. After reviewing and considering the all the papers and evidence filed in connection with this motion, the Court finds that in this case, disqualification is not necessary to protect the rights of Defendant, nor does the Court find that the integrity of the judicial proceedings has been compromised in any manner. (People ex rel Dept. of Corporations v. SpeeDee Oil Change Systems, Inc. (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1135, 1145.) To start, the facts underlining this motion are distinguishable from other cases where disqualification was warranted. For example, there has been no "use" of the privileged documents to craft a pleading or develop a new claim, (cf., Clark v. Superior Court (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 37); or used to depose a witness or party, adverse or otherwise. (Cf., McDermott Will & Emery LLP v. Superior Court (2017) 10 Cal.App.5th 1083.) On the contrary, Plaintiff's underlying theory of the case and all their allegations in support of their theory of liability have remained consistent throughout, and have been part and parcel of this litigation prior to the Clawback notice and, more importantly, since the filing of the complaint. (Complaint, 53, 54, 55, 64, 65.) Quite simply, there is no evidence that any privileged information was used in a way that affected or will affect the litigation. A trial court may not order disqualification "simply to punish a dereliction that will likely have no substantial continuing effect on future judicial proceedings." (Oaks Management Corporation v. Superior Court (2006) 145 Cal.App.4th 453, 467, quoting Gregori v. Bank of America (1989) 207 Cal.App.3d 291, 309.) The Court notes that this case has involved tens of thousands of pages of documents exchanged between counsel. By the time Defendant sent Plaintiff the Clawback notice, 7 months after producing the document, Plaintiff's counsel had already reviewed the privileged document. The Court finds that this review was before any reasonable attorney in Plaintiffs' counsel's shoes would have reason to suspect the document was privileged, or that it even may be privileged, because Plaintiff was not aware of the identity of in-house counsel, and their status as in-house counsel was not discernable by examination of the document itself. (Rico v. Mitsubishi Motors Corp. (2007) 42 Cal.4th 807, 818; State Comp. Ins. Fund v. WPS, Inc. (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 644, 656-57.) Upon receipt of the list of Defendants' in-house counsel (four months after production, and after counsel had already reviewed the document), Plaintiff continued their laborious analysis of Defendants' voluminous privilege log. Plaintiff's counsel did not re-review or search the 2,000+ documents already produced three months earlier to determine whether Defendants produced communications which included in-house counsel. After the Clawback notice, Plaintiff filed their motion under seal challenging the asserted privilege. In the context of inadvertently disclosed attorney-client communications, "[m]ere exposure" to an adversary's confidences is insufficient, standing alone, to warrant an attorney's disqualification." (State Fund, supra, 70 Cal.App.4th at 657.) Simply put, this case not an example where disqualification is justified. (Rico, supra, 42 Cal.4th at 819.) Part 1 of 2

rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITIONER CHRISTOPHER J. KAYE'S MOTION TO UNSEAL RECORDS, ELECTRONIC COURT DOCKET, AND CASE FILE
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for May 17, 2023 line 8. PETITIONER CHRISTOPHER J. KAYE'S MOTION TO UNSEAL RECORDS, ELECTRONIC COURT DOCKET, AND CASE FILE is GRANTED, in part, and DENIED, in part. Judgment, filed on July 16, 1999, Notice of Entry of Judgment, filed on July 26, 1999, and the Statement of Decision, filed on June 9, 1999, shall be unsealed. Petitioner showed an overriding interest in unsealing specific documents that are not overcome by Defendant Regina Delaney's right to privacy.
